coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional fluorescent PEGylated fluorinated graphene for targeted drug delivery: An experiment and DFT study
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Peiwei
Zhang, Lei
Yuan, Xiang-ai
Liu, Xicheng
Diao, Xiuli
Zhao, Qiao
Tian, Zhenzhen
Sun, Jing
Liu, Zhe
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: A facile strategy to synthesize multifunctional nanosized fluorinated graphene oxide (FGO), a novel nanocarrier to controllably and specifically deliver single or mixed anticancer drugs, was demonstrated. FGO was prepared by a mild process to tune its size into nanoscale and modify its surface with oxygen groups, and then functionalize with folic acid (FA) pre-linked amino-polyethylene glycol (PEG), rendering good solubility and specifical targetability toward cancer cells with FA receptors. Moreover, anticancer drugs of doxorubicin and camptothecin were effectively loaded onto FGO-PEG-FA separately or together in a well-controlled manner, and density functional theory calculations revealed that non-covalent interactions were the driving forces for drug loading and fluorine atoms provided more active sites for intermolecular interactions. Good control over surface and structure further endowed FGO unique and roust photoluminescence (PL) as well as high near infrared (NIR) absorbance. The former for the first time made FGO an excellent sensor to visually monitor drug loading by inner filter effects, while the latter guaranteed FGO an effective photothermal therapy (PTT) agent. Based on these, a three-in-one therapy model combined with mixed drug treating and PTT was constructed, and further cancer inhibition experiments revealed that remarkably higher treatment effect was achieved compared to single or two-in-one therapy. The presented work to design, synthesize and modify FGO as a novel nanocarrier and study on the interactions with drugs provided novel insights into developing new drug delivery systems, and the unique PL combined with high NIR absorbance made this work potentially valuable in many fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorine
Carbon materials
Luminescence
Structure design
Drug delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nano-sized FGO, NH2-PEG-FA and FGO-PEG-FA
Single or mixed drug loading processes
Uptake and drug targeted delivery tests

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations for synthesizing FGO-PEG-FA for targeting delivery of single or mixed anticancer drug of DOX and CPT, and its application in cancer synergistic therapy.

TEM data for (a) FG and (b) FGO. (c) Size distribution of FGO. (d) FTIR analyses of FGO, PEG-FA and FGO-PEG-FA. (e) XPS survey data for FGO and FGO-PEG-FA. (f) XRD patterns for raw material of FGi, FGO and FGO-PEG-FA.
(a) Concentration and (b) power density effects on photothermal performance of FGO-PEG-FA; (c) Warming and cooling cycle stability study at 0.2 mg/mL under 1.3 W/cm2.

(a) UV-vis absorption results for DOX, and PL emission and excitation spectra for FGO-PEG-FA; (b) The turn-off fluorescence property to monitor the drug loading process. Inset shows the fluorescence intensity changes of FGO-PEG-FA with more and more DOX being loaded onto the surface. (c) Robust PL performance of FGO-PEG-FA at different pH values.

Drug loading performance study of FGO-PEG-FA. (a) FTIR results for DOX, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX; (b) UV-vis data for DOX, FGO-PEG-FA and FGO-PEG-FA + DOX; (c) DOX loading capacity study of FGO-PEG-FA, and the insets showing the dispersibility of FGO and FGO-PEG-FA in PBS. (d–f) showing corresponding data for loading CPT on FGO-PEG-FA. The insets in (f) show the dispersibility of FGO and FGO-PEG-FA in cell culture medium.

Mixed drug loading study. (a) FTIR and (b) UV-vis results for CPT, DOX, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT. (c) Loading ratio of DOX to FGO-PEG-FA in mixed solutions of DOX and CPT, and in single DOX solution (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Calculated electrostatic potential of (a) DOX, (b) CPT and (c) FGO on the van der Waals surface. The above are the top view and the bottom are side view. Red represents negative electrostatic potential and blue represents positive one. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Reduced density gradient (RDG) isosurfaces that represent the interactions region between drugs and FGO. (a) FGO + DOX, (b) FGO + CPT and (c) FGO + CPT + DOX.

Flow cytometry data obtained by incubating samples in (a) HeLa cells (DOX concentration, 15 μg/mL) Cell uptake comparison between HeLa and A549 cell at different drug doses of (b) 10 μg/mL and (c) 20 μg/mL.
Laser scanning confocal microscope study of FGO-PEG-FA + DOX after being incubated in HeLa cells for (a) 0.5 and (b) 4 h.

24 h cell toxicity of FGO and FGO-PEG-FA for (a) HeLa and (b) BEAS-2B cell; Viability of HeLa cells incubated in DOX, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX and FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + NIR for (c) 24 h and (d) 48 h. (e) Viability of HeLa cells treated by DOX, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + NIR, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT and FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT + NIR; (f) Scheme illustration for synergetic therapy effects that combine chemotherapy using Top 1 and Top 2 inhibitor and PTT.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional fluorescent PEGylated fluorinated graphene for targeted drug delivery: An experiment and DFT study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis
Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining, 810001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining, 810008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile strategy to synthesize multifunctional nanosized fluorinated graphene oxide (FGO), a novel nanocarrier to controllably and specifically deliver single or mixed anticancer drugs, was demonstrated. FGO was prepared by a mild process to tune its size into nanoscale and modify its surface with oxygen groups, and then functionalize with folic acid (FA) pre-linked amino-polyethylene glycol (PEG), rendering good solubility and specifical targetability toward cancer cells with FA receptors. Moreover, anticancer drugs of doxorubicin and camptothecin were effectively loaded onto FGO-PEG-FA separately or together in a well-controlled manner, and density functional theory calculations revealed that non-covalent interactions were the driving forces for drug loading and fluorine atoms provided more active sites for intermolecular interactions. Good control over surface and structure further endowed FGO unique and roust photoluminescence (PL) as well as high near infrared (NIR) absorbance. The former for the first time made FGO an excellent sensor to visually monitor drug loading by inner filter effects, while the latter guaranteed FGO an effective photothermal therapy (PTT) agent. Based on these, a three-in-one therapy model combined with mixed drug treating and PTT was constructed, and further cancer inhibition experiments revealed that remarkably higher treatment effect was achieved compared to single or two-in-one therapy. The presented work to design, synthesize and modify FGO as a novel nanocarrier and study on the interactions with drugs provided novel insights into developing new drug delivery systems, and the unique PL combined with high NIR absorbance made this work potentially valuable in many fields.

A multifunctional concept of nanocarrier based on fluorescent PEGylated fluorinated graphene was constructed, and its first application in targeted delivery of mixed anticancer drugs was studied by both experiment and DFT calculations.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Research and development of effective cancer therapy strategy has been highly urgent since cancer remains the prominent cause of death in the world through the ages [1–3]. The disadvantages of conventional single-model therapy, such as poor bioavailability, unsatisfactory targeting specificity, and strong side effects etc., are becoming more and more obvious [4–7]. To resolve these issues, combined therapy including chemotherapy, photodynamic therapy (PDT), photothermal therapy (PTT) and radiation therapy etc. has been attracting increasing attention due to its higher efficiency and lower recurrence rate [8–10]. To achieve combined therapy, multifunctional nano-materials are becoming a hot-spot because of their efficient drug loading, targeted delivery, and controlled drug release [11–14]. Therefore, increasing effort is being devoted to design and construct multifunctional drug delivery systems (DDS) with encouraging cancer therapy effects.

On the other hand, although chemotherapy is still an indispensable auxiliary treatment, its effect has been far from satisfactory, mostly attributing to drug delivery problems resulting from physiological barriers and drug resistance [15–17]. To address the physiological barrier issue, the DDS should possess proper surface and size for blood circulation and tumor accumulation, and it is found that DDS with sizes of 50–100 nm are appropriate for permeability and retention. And to decrease drug resistance, combined chemotherapy that uses two or multiple anticancer drugs is gradually chosen as a new therapeutic model in clinic. Combined chemotherapy can modulate different signal pathways and induce synergetic response [18–20], which finally maximizes therapy effects and overcomes drug resistance. Thus it would be of great significance to construct DDS that are multifunctional for combined therapy, and at the same time can specifically deliver mixed drugs to achieve better therapeutic outcome.

The newly discovered fluorinated graphene (FG) nanosheets are providing arising opportunities to design conceptually new biological devices. As fluorine covering the surface, FG can serve as a novel metal-free magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast based on 19F [21–23], and induce positively biological responses due to the high electronegativity of fluorine atoms [24–26] Meanwhile, FG inherits high specific surface area of graphene [27–29] and drugs can be loaded onto both sides, all of which guarantee high drug loading capacity. Moreover, the unique electronic structure makes FG an effective photosensitizer that exhibits higher near-infrared (NIR) photothermal property for PTT [30,31]. Unfortunately, also because of fluorine, FG is highly hydrophobic and chemically inert [32–35]. The former makes it hard to be dispersed and thus no drugs can be loaded, while the latter makes it difficult to be modified by tumor target molecules and cut into proper sizes (50–100 nm). Therefore, few works are reported to employ FG as a multifunctional therapy platform to specifically deliver mixed drugs with controlled ratio, and the interactions between FG and single or mixed drugs remain unexplored.

To resolve this challenge, we present an interesting concept of FG-based DDS with bright photoluminescence (PL) to visually monitor drug loading, high NIR absorption for PTT, and special cancer targeting for mixed drugs delivery. The interactions between FG and different drugs are systematically investigated and uncovered by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. As showed in Fig. 1 , to construct this DDS, large FG sheets were adjusted into nanosized ones (Ca. 60 nm) for easier cell uptake, and simultaneously modified with oxygen groups (namely fluorinated graphene oxide, FGO) for further targeting modification. NH2-modified polyethylene glycol (NH2-PEG-NH2) was first linked with folic acid (FA, a tumor target molecule), and then grafted onto FGO surface, rendering good solubility in physiological solutions and readily loading of both camptothecin (CPT, water-insoluble) and doxorubicin (DOX, water-soluble) in well-controlled manners. The mixed drug loaded nanocarrier could be selectively uptaken by cancer cells with FA receptors, and exhibited much better therapeutic effect compared with single-drug therapy. Meanwhile, nanosized FGO-PEG-FA enjoyed high NIR absorption to effectively photoablating cancer cells, and had negligible toxicity at concentrations well above the ones needed for PTT. More importantly, nanosized FGO-PEG-FA also exhibited bright and robust PL, and its absorption well overlapped the emission curves of drugs, which then emerged as a novel visual sensor to monitor the drug loading process by inner filter effect (IFE). This work reported an effective strategy to controllably adjust the size, chemical surface and property of FG, and established it as a novel and multifunctional material for drug loading, targeted delivery, PL, PTT, and effective cancer combined therapy.

The effective control over structure and chemical surface was first investigated by TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 a, large and transparent FG sheets with corrugated surface were obtained. After the designed oxidation and cutting treatment, it was found that much smaller FGO sheets with an average size of ∼60 nm were synthesized (Fig. 2 b and c). Compared to FG and FGi (Fig. S1), FTIR data (Fig. 2 d) revealed that typical CF bonds (1211 cm−1) were retained on FGO [41], and oxygen groups in the forms of CO (1074 cm−1) and COOH (1730 cm−1) were also introduced, which served as reaction sites for further targeted modification. Moreover, FTIR data showed that different from FA and H2N-PEG-NH2 (Fig. S2), obvious HNCO bonds (1636 cm−1) appeared on H2N-PEG-FA [38], indicating successful link of FA onto H2N-PEG-NH2 after amidation reaction. And after further reaction with FGO, it was found that typical bonds for FA located at 1605 and 1692 cm−1, and apparent HNCO bonds located at 1640 cm−1 were detected on FGO-PEG-FA, confirming that amide bonds between PEG-FA (NH2) and FGO (COOH) was formed. This result was further validated by XPS analyses from Fig. 2e. It was observed that only C (285.4 eV), O (532.1 eV) and F (688.9 eV) elements could be detected on pure FGO, while for FGO-PEG-FA, besides these three elements, prominent N elements from H2N-PEG-FA located at 400.6 eV was also observed, suggesting the effective covalent bonds between them. Besides these chemical and morphological analyses, XRD measurements were also conducted. For FGi, the peak located 14.6° at was ascribed to the (001) pattern, a reflection of carbon materials with high fluorine contents. And peaks located at 26.8° and 40.7° were ascribed to (002) and (100) pattern in a classical graphene-like structure [42]. And for FGO, it was found that the (001) peak moved to 10.1° after oxidation, revealing that the exfoliation and oxidation process made the interlamellar spacing become larger. And for FGO-PEG-FA, it was observed that besides the diffraction peaks of FGO, typical peaks for PEG located at 19.3° and 23.5° also appeared [43], further demonstrating the successful modification process.

The changes of size and surface chemistry also allowed greatly enhanced photothermal performance. It was found that compared with graphene oxide and FG with large sizes (Fig. S3), the prepared FGO possessed higher photothermal conversion property, in which the temperature of FGO solution could rapidly reach upto 65 °C. Further study (Fig. 3 a) indicated that a concentration-dependent temperature rise behavior was observed, and when FGO-PEG-FA concentration was 0.1 mg/mL, temperature reached 43 °C, a temperature that was suggested to effectively enhance drug's toxicity toward cancer cells [44]. It was also observed that with concentration or power density increasing (Fig. 3b), temperature of FGO-PEG-FA solution rapidly increased to more than 60 °C at relatively low concentration (0.2 mg/mL) and power density (1.7 W/cm2). Moreover, due to the chemical stability of FGO, FGO-PEG-FA solution kept nice photothermal responses even after ten cycles of on/off irradiation (Fig. 3c), guaranteeing good reliability for its further practical applications.

Besides the novel photothermal property, adjusting the size into nanoscale also endowed FGO-PEG-FA with robust photoluminescence (PL), which could be further employed to monitor the drug loading process due to inner filter effect (IFE). IFE has emerged as an effective method for sensor design, and it is based on the mechanism that in a detection system, when absorber's absorption spectra overlap with fluorophore's excitation or emission spectra, the fluorophore's luminescence can be well quenched and thus it can transfer absorption responses into fluorescent signals [45]. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, FGO-PEG-FA exhibited strong blue emission centered at 430 nm under excitation of 365 nm, and the PL excitation (PLE) spectrum under the monitor of 430 nm showed a strong peak at 365 nm. For doxorubicin (DOX), a broad-spectrum anticancer drug that had been widely used in clinic to treat cancers, had strong absorption ranging from 400 to 550 nm, which well overlapped the emission curve of FGO-PEG-FA and thus its luminescence could be quenched by DOX. To study this effect, different amounts of DOX were gradually added into FGO-PEG-FA solutions, and it was found that the PL intensity gradually decreased with more and more DOX being absorbed on the surface. And the inset images visually displayed the luminescence changes of FGO-PEG-FA from bright blue emission (0 mg/mL of DOX) to totally quenched (0.32 mg/mL of DOX). Accordingly, it was also expected that when the drugs were released from FGO-PEG-FA surface, the luminescence should be recovered. This speculation was well validated by the drug release experiments (Fig. S4). With more and more drugs being released, the luminescence of the nanocarrier was gradually recovered. Different from using UV-Vis spectrophotometer to detect the drug loading or release process, this novel IFE based method allowed FGO-PEG-FA to serve as a new platform to visually detect drug loading by a colorized manner with naked eyes. More importantly, it was found that no obvious PL intensity changes of FGO-PEG-FA were observed when pH values was adjusted from 1 to 12 (Fig. 4c), showing very stable PL performance even under strong acid or alkali conditions. This uncommon PL behavior of FGO-PEG-FA was ascribed to the modified fluorine and long polymer chains on the nanosheets, which protected FGO from being protonation or affected by the solvents and finally resulted into relatively unchanged PL under different conditions. Specially, stable PL under acid conditions was very important for bioimaging agents because most intracellular conditions of cancer cells were acid, and it was worthy noticing that most luminescence of the reported carbon-based materials usually quenched or greatly degraded when pH value was lower than 6 [46,47]. The stable PL of FGO-PEG-FA made it a valuable bioimaging agent under different conditions and we found that it could serve as novel and stable fluorochrome in HeLa cells (Fig. S5).

FGO possessed high polyaromatic surface and enjoyed good solubility in various media (the insets in Fig. 5 c, f) after being modified by H2N-PEG-FA, and thus it was considered that FGO-PEG-FA should have high drug loading capacity because drugs could be loaded onto both sides. To study this property, DOX, a clinically used water-soluble anticancer was chosen and readily loaded onto FGO-PEG-FA by stirring them overnight (see details in experiment part), and any unbounded drugs were removed by filter, repeated centrifugation. FTIR data in Fig. 5a indicated that typical stretching vibration absorption peaks of DOX located at 1585 cm−1 and 1619 cm−1 were obviously detected from FGO-PEG-FA + DOX [48], indicating effective loading of DOX onto FGO-PEG-FA. The result was further validated by UV-vis data in Fig. 5b, characteristic peak of FA located at 278 nm was detected on FGO-PEG-FA, and for pure DOX, its characteristic peaks were centered at 232 nm and 483 nm. These absorption peaks were readily detected from UV-vis curves of FGO-PEG-FA + DOX, which further confirmed the successful targeting modification and effective drug loading. Moreover, drug-loading ratio (the weight ratio of DOX loaded to FGO-PEG-FA) was also measured by the absorbance at 483 nm for DOX after subtracting FGO-PEG-FA absorbance. It was observed that a very high loading ratio of 350% was obtained when drug concentration reached 2 mg/mL, and it was the highest drug loading ratio for FG that had been reported. Besides DOX, camptothecin (CPT), a water-insoluble anticancer drug was also chosen to investigate the drug loading performance of FGO-PEG-FA. CPT was loaded by mixing FGO-PEG-FA with a solution of CPT in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and unbound CPT or excess DMSO was removed by centrifugation and dialysis. It was found that pure CPT showed typical peaks at 1156, 1596 and 1742 cm−1, and these peaks were also detected from FGO-PEG-FA + CPT (Fig. 5d) [38], revealing effective CPT loading. Moreover, this result was further validated by UV-vis data in Fig. 5e, in which characteristic peaks of CPT located at 252, 361, and 375 nm were also found from the UV-vis curves of FGO-PEG-FA + CPT. Drug-loading ratio of CPT was then studied and it was observed that the loading ratio could reach 40%, a value higher than graphene-related materials. These convincing data demonstrate that the constructed FGO-PEG-FA is a powerful nanocarrier that can load both hydrophobic and hydrophilic anticancer drugs with very high contents, and this further encourages us to study whether it can load mixed drugs with opposite solubility and anticancer mechanisms, which is of great significance in clinical to overcome drug resistance and improve therapeutic effect.

To study the mixed drug loading property, the key problem remaining to resolve is how to design the loading process which considers the solubility of CPT and DOX and allows fine control over the drug loading ratio. Due to their differences in chemical structure, the interactions between the two drugs and FGO-PEG-FA are different, resulting into the fact that the loading conditions and ratio of the two drugs are different. On the other hand, as showed in Fig. 5c and f, the loading ratios of both drugs were linearly correlated to the concentrations, and this made well-controlled loading promising. To achieve mixed drug loading, a two-step drug loading process was employed. CPT was first loaded onto FGO-PEG-FA using the method for single CPT loading (see the experimental part), and then different amounts of DOX were added into the mixture for further drug loading. Unbound or undissolved drugs were removed through several centrifugation and washing, and final FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT products were purified by dialysis and collected by lyophilization. As showed in FTIR data (Fig. 6 a), characteristic peaks for DOX (1623 cm−1) and CPT (1596 cm−1) were detected on FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT, indicating that both drugs were loaded. Similar results were also validated by UV-vis data in Fig. 6b, in which typical absorption peaks at 485 nm (DOX) and 375 nm (CPT) of FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT also demonstrated successful mixed drug loading. The encouraging result then further urged us to study whether it was possible to load DOX and CPT in a fine controlled manner and achieve tunable drug loading. To investigate this, we changed the concentrations of DOX from 0.01 to 0.1 mg/mL and kept the CPT concentration constant (ca. 0.1 mg/mL) to study relationship between the DOX loading ratio and drug concentrations. It was found that although DOX concentration changed, the CPT loading ratio was nearly constant (about 10.5%) while DOX's loading ratio increased linearly with increasing DOX concentrations, similar to single DOX loading behavior in Fig. 5c. In consideration of the loading ratio of either drug was similar to that in single drug loading process (Fig. 5c,f), our experimental findings suggested that the loading behavior of either CPT or DOX was mostly affected by their distribution coefficient in solutions and that absorbed on FGO-PEG-FA surface. Therefore, the loading amount of DOX or CPT on FGO-PEG-FA could be well adjusted by changing the drug ratios in solution, which was practically important for clinical application of FGO-PEG-FA in loading multiple drugs in controlled ratio. Although the synthesized FGO-PEG-FA can load single or mixed drugs in a well-controlled manner, yet how the drug molecular interacts with the nanocarrier?

Previous works pointed out that hydrogen bond and van der Waals (vdW) interactions were main driving forces for loading drugs onto carbon materials like CNTs or graphene [49,50]. However, in view of chemical composition, these materials only possessed C, H and O three elements and the electronegativity gap between C and O was not large. For FGO, it possessed both O and F on the surface and F atom was suggested to have the largest electronegativity among all the elements. So interactions between FGO and CPT or DOX were more complex, and to the best of knowledge, no related works have been reported to explain the mechanism. Herein, in order to explore interaction for drug loading, both electrostatic potential (ESP) and reduced density gradient (RDG) isosurface based on DFT calculations were employed to study the mechanism. ESP distributions on the molecule surfaces are affected by the chemical groups and geometrical features, and can objectively and visually show electrostatic properties of molecules. As displayed in Fig. 7 , large and negative regions (in red) on ESP surface of DOX were located around oxygen and nitrogen atoms, which resulted from their strong electronegativity to attract electrons from carbon or hydrogen atoms. Accordingly, most positive regions (in blue) were observed around hydrogen atoms. Similar results were also found on CPT and FGO, in which most negative regions on the ESP surface were detected around oxygen, nitrogen and fluorine atoms. It was known that monomers often contacted each other on the ESP surface in a complementary manner. They always canceled each other out and finally made the complex stable.

To study the interactions between drugs and FGO, RDG analyses were carried out and various types of intermolecular interactions between them were identified by the color-filled RDG isosurface. As showed in Fig. 8 and according to the color scale bar, deep blue (red) indicated stronger interactions and green suggests vdW interactions. The elliptical slab (light blue) between oxygen (or fluorine) and hydrogen atoms corresponded to hydrogen bonds. As for FGO and DOX (Fig. 8a), the interaction regions (light green) between carbon/fluorine and hydrogen represented vdW interactions, and this suggested that electron densities in these regions were low. Meanwhile, the regions (red) in the center of the carbon rings revealed strong steric effect in this system. The intermolecular interaction between them was calculated as −16.2 kcal/mol, and for FGO-CPT system, the interactions between them were a little weaker and a value of −11.6 kcal/mol was observed. This indicated that although the interactions between DOX or CPT and FGO were similar, due to the lack of oxygen groups, interaction in FGO-CPT system was weaker than that in FGO-DOX system. When both DOX and CPT were loaded onto FGO surface, it was found that the interactions among them were more complex. Besides vdW interactions between drugs and FGO, obvious hydrogen bonds formed among oxygen, nitrogen, fluorine and hydrogen. And compared to GO, introduction of fluorine atoms provided more active sites for intermolecular interactions between drugs and FGO. This investigation revealed that FGO could serve as an effective drug delivery via physical adsorption, i.e. non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonding, van der Waals interactions, etc.) to load water-soluble and water-insoluble drugs, and it was worthy noticing that such interactions were very important not only in drug design, but also in materials science and molecular recognition with RNA or DNA molecules.

To study the cell uptake and targeting effects, flow cytometry (FC) measurement was conducted. FC is considered as a powerful technology to accurately measure protein or nucleic acid in cells [39], and considering the bright fluorescence of DOX (Figure S6), the cell uptake and targeting specificity processes of FGO-PEG-FA were investigated by measuring DOX fluorescence in cells. As show in Fig. 9 a, obvious fluorescence signal of DOX was detected in HeLa cells incubated with pure DOX and FGO-PEG-FA + DOX, revealing that the nanocarrier was effectively uptaken into cells and the drugs readily released from its surface. To investigate the targeting selectivity toward cancer cells, HeLa and A549 cells were chosen because the former were positive FA receptors while the latter were negative ones [51,52]. The data in Fig. 9b demonstrated that under same conditions, much stronger fluorescence signal was detected in HeLa cells, whose fluorescence intensity was about 4.2 times greater than that of A549 cells. And similar results were also found when cells were incubated with a higher DOX concentration (Fig. 9c). This fact clearly proved that the specific targeting of FGO-PEG-FA toward HeLa cells, and this was further confirmed in Fig. 11 and Fig. S7. This effective cell uptake and intracellular drug release property were further visually validated by laser scanning confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 10 a, DOX fluorescence signal in HeLa cells incubated with FGO-PEG-FA + DOX for 0.5 h was weak, and was not overlapped with DAPI's blue fluorescence signal (a bioimaging agents commonly used to label nucleus). This indicated that FGO-PEG-FA + DOX had just been endocytosed and thus most DOX was not released, and accordingly the fluorescence was weak. After 4 h (Fig. 10b), it was found that DOX's red fluorescence signal became stronger and distributed in cytosol as well as nucleus, demonstrating the successful drug release that was triggered by the intracellular acid conditions. It was known that the endocytosis process was mostly depended on energy, relating to temperature or adenosine triphosphate [49], and for carbon materials like carbon nanotubes (CNTs), it was considered that their cell endocytosis process was dependent on clathrin [50,51]. As FGO possessed similar graphic surface to CNTs, it was speculated that FGO's uptake process was also by this pathway, and much less adenosine triphosphate was needed to uptake smaller FGO sheets compared to larger ones in micro-scale. Furthermore, considering its high PL, NIR absorption and potential use as MRI contrast (based on 19F), as well as the ability to load mixed drugs, FGO was expected to serve as a novel multifunctional nanomaterial with promising applications in imaging, PTT, drug or gene delivery.

As the constructed FGO-PEG-FA + DOX system exhibited good photothermal property, novel PL performance, high drug loading ratio and targeting specificity, next we investigated its chemo-photothermal therapy effects. To be a safe nanocarrier, cytotoxicity is the top concern. So toxicity of both FGO and FGO-PEG-FA was first studied (Fig. 11 a,b). Both of them showed negligible toxicity toward BEAS-2B cells (normal cells) and HeLa cells (cancer cells) even if under very high doses (150 μg/mL), and it was worthy noticing that under the same dose, FGO-PEG-FA exhibited obviously higher cell viability, indicating that modification of FGO could further improve the biocompatibility. After incubating cells with different samples under the same conditions for 24 h (Fig. 11c), it was found that viability of HeLa cells treated by FGO-PEG-FA + DOX was higher than free DOX. This was mostly because that free DOX diffused much more easily through cell membrane than FGO-PEG-FA [37], and this trend was totally reversed when an NIR irradiation was added. It was observed that when a combination of drug and PTT was employed, the viability of HeLa cells was greatly reduced and lower than free DOX. The greatly improved therapy effects became more obvious with drug doses increased or incubation time prolonged to 48 h (Fig. 11d), which a very low cell viability (20%) was readily obtained. To study the cancer therapy effects under even lower drug doses and evaluate the mixed drug treatment with NIR, a more careful cell experiment was conducted. As showed in Fig. 11d, It was found that FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + NIR still showed much higher cancer inhibition than DOX or FGO-PEG-FA + DOX even at very low drug doses (from 1 to 4.5 μg/mL), revealing that the constructed FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + NIR enjoyed greatly enhanced cancer therapeutic effects. Meanwhile, it was also observed that a mixed drug treatment (FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT) under the same conditions showed satisfactory cancer cell inhibition effects, a low cell viability that was comparable to FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + NIR. So, there was no doubt that when NIR laser was added, FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT + NIR exhibited the best cancer therapeutic effect among all the tested samples. As displayed in Fig. 11 e, this three-in-one therapeutic pattern showed more than 30% cell inhibition compared with pure DOX or FGO-PEG-FA + DOX, and nearly 30% cell inhibition compared with FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + NIR or FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT. This result guaranteed promising application in clinics, which was expected to employ as few drugs as possible to achieve better therapeutic effects while reducing side toxicity.

This excellent cancer therapeutic effect was mostly attributed to the following reasons: one was the strategy of using mixed drugs, which maximized the pesticide effects and reduced the influence of drug resistance on cancer cells. It was known that two main topoisomerases were distributed in human cells and induced single or double-strand DNA break [53], respectively. Topoisomerase I (top 1) and II (top 2) inhibitors could stabilize transient enzyme-DNA cleavable complex and finally poisoned those enzymes, which further resulted into DNA break and cell deaths. And CPT (top 1 inhibitor) and DOX (top 2 inhibitor) were different topoisomerase inhibitors, and they had different DNA binding sites and thus played different roles in cell cycles. They killed cancer cells in distinct manners and accordingly were more effective in cancer inhibition. Besides this, the other reason for the excellent cancer therapeutic effects was the temperature effect. It has been reported that drugs like DOX was more toxic toward cells at temperature above 43 °C. Additionally, temperature of FGO-PEG-FA with NIR under the same conditions was nearly 50 °C and such temperature could also kill cancer cells by hyperthermia. Therefore, this novel integrated FGO-PEG-FA + DOX + CPT + NIR system worked as a multifunctional nanocarrier for effectively synergetic therapy, making full use of mixed drug chemotherapy, PTT, NIR improved drug poisonousness to inhibit cancer cell in more effective manners. A scheme illustration to explain and summarize these was displayed in Fig. 11f.

In summary, we demonstrated a novel FGO-based nanocarrier with nanosized structure, modifiable surface, high PL emission, high drug loading capacity and NIR absorption, and detailed DFT investigations on the interactions of FGO with single or mixed drugs were studied. Successful control over structure endowed FGO with greatly improved photothermal conversion, and unique luminescence to visually monitor drug loading and serve as a stable bioimaging agent. Effective modification of FGO with biocompatible PEG and FA realized low toxicity, easy uptake, and targeted delivery of mixed drugs for the first time. Notably enhanced cancer growth inhibition was obtained at very low drug concentrations under this novel three-in-one therapy manner. This work reported a new train of thought to utilize fluorinated carbon material in biomedicine, and provided a new strategy to design targeted nanocarrier to delivery single or mixed drugs with controlled ratio and a novel highly effective photothermal therapy agent, which was expected to reduce drug doses in therapy, overcome drug resistance and improve therapeutic effect to the most content in future applications.
The materials and reagents used in the experiments were presented in Part 1.1 in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nano-sized FGO, NH2-PEG-FA and FGO-PEG-FA
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesis of FGO, fluorinated graphite was first exfoliated and activated by an F-sacrificing strategy and then cut into nano-sized ones based on our previous works with modifications to introduce oxygen and keep fluorine [36,37]. Details for the activation and oxidation processes were presented in 1.2 in supporting information.

To synthesize NH2-PEG-FA, NH2-modified PEG (H2N-PEG-NH2) was used and thus FA could be linked into the biomolecule by amidating reactions between COOH of FA and NH2 of H2N-PEG-NH2. After reactions, the product solution was dialyzed for two days and changed the water every 5 h during this process. Then, the first yellow fraction was lyophilized. To synthesize FGO-PEG-FA, a similar process was conducted and the difference was that the reaction was between COOH (from FGO) and NH2 (from NH2-PEG-FA). Details for the processes were presented in 1.3 in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single or mixed drug loading processes
ce:section.ce:para: After being modified by oxygen and PEG-FA, DOX or CPT can be readily loaded by simply mixing solutions under controlled pH and drug concentrations for fixed time. Unloaded drugs were removed through filter and centrifugations, and products were finally obtained by vacuum freeze drying. Mixed loading CPT and DOX were achieved based on previous works with some modifications [38]. And the details can be found in part 1.4 in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and drug targeted delivery tests
ce:section.ce:para: To study cell uptake and targeting toward cancer cell overexpressed FA receptors, flow cytometer tests were conducted and the intracellular behavior were studied based on our previously established methods [39,40]. This could be referred to part 1.5 in supporting information.

The intracellular laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images were conducted to investigate the uptake of the nanocarrier and distribution of DOX. To see the action site of DOX, the cell nuclei were stained by DAPI for comparison. Details for sample preparation, cell tests and experiment parameter were supplied in Part 1.6 in supporting information.

Cancer therapy experiments were carried out using HeLa and BEAS-2B cell lines. To compare targeted delivery effects, singe therapy and combined therapy, and control experiments were set and studied. Details for all the cell experiment were provided in 1.7 in supporting information.
Equipment and method used to investigate the chemistry surface, composition structure, fluorescence, PTT, drug load and cell behaviors, etc. could be found in Part 1.8 in supporting information.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018QB002, ZR2017MB038) and Shandong Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J16LA03, J18KA082) of China. We also thanks the Taishan Scholars and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21671118, 21703118).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Revised Supplementary material Revised Supplementary material

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.10.031.
